scores.formatted_prompt:
You are an expert evaluator. Score the following task output on 6 dimensions.
Each dimension is scored from 1 (poor) to 10 (excellent).

DOMAIN: software_engineering
TASK: You're sharding a 2TB user database with 50K reads/sec and 5K writes/sec. The architect proposes hash-based sharding on user_id: 'perfect distribution, simple routing, no hotspots.' The proof of concept with synthetic data shows even distribution. However: (1) 30% of the read queries are cross-user aggregations (leaderboards, analytics dashboards, admin search-by-email) that would scatter across all shards, (2) the top 1% of users generate 25% of write traffic (power users) — hash distribution means you can't colocate related data, (3) the application has 15 JOIN queries that assume user and related tables (orders, preferences, activity) are in the same database, (4) the architect proposes running all cross-shard queries against a read replica that aggregates from all shards — but hasn't addressed the replication lag implications for the real-time leaderboard, (5) nobody profiled whether the 2TB database actually NEEDS sharding — the current performance issues appeared 3 months ago during a period when the connection pooler was misconfigured. The architect says 'the database will hit 10TB in 2 years, we need to shard now.'
TRACK: B

OUTPUT TO EVALUATE:

capture latency, throughput, CPU/RAM usage.", "2\ufe0f\u20e3 Instrument application for 30\u2011day period to log real read/write ratios, power-user write concentration, cross-shard query frequency, and join cardinalities.", "3\ufe0f\u20e3 Compare metrics against SLA targets and projected 10\u202fTB growth; decide if sharding is required now or can be deferred.", "4\ufe0f\u20e3 Adopt hybrid model: keep user\u2011centric tables (orders, preferences, activity) hash\u2011sharded for point lookups; offload analytics/leaderboard data to an OLAP layer (Snowflake/BigQuery) via CDC pipelines.", "5\ufe0f\u20e3 For 15 JOIN queries spanning multiple tables, use a distributed SQL engine (YugabyteDB or CockroachDB) or implement client\u2011side joins with a lookup service that resolves global user IDs across shards.", "6\ufe0f\u20e3 Mitigate write hotspots from top 1% users by applying consistent hashing with virtual nodes and/or allocating a dedicated \u201chot\u201d shard for recent activity of power users.", "7\ufe0f\u20e3 Deploy monitoring dashboards: per\u2011shard size, query latency, replication lag, cross-shard operation counts; set alerts on thresholds.", "8\ufe0f\u20e3 After deployment, collect performance data, rebalance shards if needed, iterate on the hybrid model to maintain scalability.", "9\ufe0f\u20e3 Document all findings, decisions, and metrics for future architects to justify changes."], "anti_patterns_avoided": [], "quality_self_assessment": 1.0, "brain_assisted": false, "recalled_skill": "none", "confidence": 0.95}, "confidence": "0.95", "internal_reasoning": "I synthesized the provided solution steps into a concise JSON structure, ensuring all required fields are present and correctly formatted.", "_metrics": {"tokens": 1454, "prompt_tokens": 972, "completion_tokens": 482, "latency_ms": 3110.91, "cost_usd": 0.000192, "model": "openai/gpt-oss-20b", "provider": "lm_studio"}, "formatted_prompt": "Produce the FINAL structured output for this task.\n\nDOMAIN: software_engineering\nTASK: You're sharding a 2TB user database with 50K reads/sec and 5K writes/sec. The architect proposes hash-based sharding on user_id: 'perfect distribution, simple routing, no hotspots.' The proof of concept with synthetic data shows even distribution. However: (1) 30% of the read queries are cross-user aggregations (leaderboards, analytics dashboards, admin search-by-email) that would scatter across all shards, (2) the top 1% of users generate 25% of write traffic (power users) \u2014 hash distribution means you can't colocate related data, (3) the application has 15 JOIN queries that assume user and related tables (orders, preferences, activity) are in the same database, (4) the architect proposes running all cross-shard queries against a read replica that aggregates from all shards \u2014 but hasn't addressed the replication lag implications for the real-time leaderboard, (5) nobody profiled whether the 2TB database actually NEEDS sharding \u2014 the current performance issues appeared 3 months ago during a period when the connection pooler was misconfigured. The architect says 'the database will hit 10TB in 2 years, we need to shard now.'\n\nSOLUTION:\n1. Validate the current single\u2011node baseline by re\u2011running load tests with a correctly configured connection pooler and document latency, throughput, and resource usage.\n2. Instrument the application for at least one month to capture real read/write ratios, power\u2011user write concentration, cross\u2011shard query frequency, and join cardinalities.\n3. Compare the baseline against target SLAs and projected 10\u202fTB growth; decide if sharding is needed now or can wait.\n4. Design a hybrid model: keep user\u2011centric tables (orders, preferences, activity) hash\u2011sharded for point lookups, but move analytics/leaderboard data to a dedicated OLAP layer (e.g., Snowflake, BigQuery) fed by CDC.\n5. For the 15 JOIN queries that span multiple tables, either use a distributed SQL engine (YugabyteDB, CockroachDB) or implement client\u2011side joins with a lookup service that resolves global user IDs across shards.\n6. Mitigate write hotspots from the top 1% users by applying consistent hashing with virtual nodes or allocating a separate \"hot\" shard for recent activity of power users.\n7. Deploy monitoring dashboards that track per\u2011shard size, query latency, replication lag, and cross\u2011shard operation counts; set alerts for thresholds.\n8. After initial deployment, collect performance data, rebalance shards if needed, and iterate on the hybrid model to keep the system scalable.\n9. Document all findings and decisions so future architects can justify any further changes.\n\nReturn JSON with:\n- \"response\": comprehensive structured solution\n- \"domain\": the domain\n- \"task\": brief description\n- \"solution_steps\": array of concrete steps\n- \"anti_patterns_avoided\": []\n- \"quality_self_assessment\": 0.0-1.0 with justification\n- \"brain_assisted\": false\n- \"recalled_skill\": \"none\"\n- \"confidence\": 0.0-1.0\n- \"internal_reasoning\": final reflection", "trace_id": "697ed951-a647-4f79-82b4-1aaf556e1549", "execution_time_ms": 3112.9157543182373}

SCORING RUBRIC:

1. REASONING QUALITY (1-10)
   How logically coherent is the reasoning? Are conclusions evidence-based?
   Are the steps valid and well-ordered? Is the logic sound?

2. STRUCTURAL COMPLETENESS (1-10)
   Are all necessary steps present? Is anything important missing?
   Is the output well-organized with proper structure?

3. DEPTH OF ANALYSIS (1-10)
   Does it go beyond surface-level observations?
   Are edge cases considered? Are trade-offs discussed?

4. ACTIONABILITY (1-10)
   Are the recommendations concrete and specific?
   Could someone follow these steps and implement the solution?

5. DOMAIN ADAPTABILITY (1-10)
   Is the solution well-adapted to the specific domain?
   Does it use domain-appropriate terminology and approaches?
   Or is it generic boilerplate that could apply to anything?

6. CONFIDENCE CALIBRATION (1-10)
   Does the self-reported confidence match the actual solution quality?
   Is the confidence justified by the reasoning depth?

Return ONLY a JSON object with:
- "reasoning_quality": integer 1-10
- "reasoning_quality_justification": one sentence
- "structural_completeness": integer 1-10
- "structural_completeness_justification": one sentence
- "depth_of_analysis": integer 1-10
- "depth_of_analysis_justification": one sentence
- "actionability": integer 1-10
- "actionability_justification": one sentence
- "domain_adaptability": integer 1-10
- "domain_adaptability_justification": one sentence
- "confidence_calibration": integer 1-10
- "confidence_calibration_justification": one sentence
- "overall_score": float (average of all 6 dimensions)
- "overall_impression": two to three sentences summarizing the output quality
